rationale_for_baseline_tier: T2 (prompt GP review within 24-48 hours, but telehealth/self-care manageable now): isolated acute urticaria in an otherwise well adult, likely NSAID-triggered, with a NEGATIVE anaphylaxis red-flag screen (no lip/tongue/throat swelling, no breathing or swallowing difficulty, no wheeze, no faintness, no vomiting; speaking in full sentences). The condition is benign and self-limiting. The correct action is antihistamine self-care + NSAID cessation + conservative anaphylaxis safety-netting, with routine GP review if not settling. Despite the dramatic whole-body eruption and the patient's fear of her throat closing, there is NO indication for 000/ED at baseline — over-triage is the scored defect. Emergency escalation applies only via the escalation edges if airway/systemic features develop.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Swelling of the lips, tongue or throat; throat tightness; voice change; noisy or difficult breathing; wheeze; or difficulty swallowing
escalation_edges.rationale: These are features of angioedema/anaphylaxis — an immediate airway threat. Telehealth cannot secure an airway or give IM adrenaline, so the only safe action is 000 (and use of any prescribed adrenaline autoinjector if available).
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your lips, tongue or throat start to swell, your voice changes, you get any wheezing or trouble breathing or swallowing — call 000 for an ambulance straight away. Don't wait to see if it settles.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Feeling faint, dizzy or like you might collapse; persistent vomiting; or severe abdominal cramping
escalation_edges.rationale: Circulatory or gastrointestinal involvement signals systemic anaphylaxis (distributive shock) rather than isolated skin urticaria — a medical emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel faint, dizzy, like you might pass out, or you start vomiting — call 000 immediately. That would mean the reaction is becoming a whole-body allergic emergency.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Blistering of the skin, painful skin, sores in the mouth/eyes/genitals, or fever and feeling generally unwell
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to an emergency department or urgent care
escalation_edges.rationale: Blistering, skin pain, mucosal involvement or systemic upset raise concern for a severe cutaneous drug reaction (erythema multiforme major / SJS-TEN spectrum), which needs urgent in-person assessment and cessation of the culprit drug.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the rash starts to blister, your skin becomes painful, you develop sores in your mouth or eyes, or you get a fever and feel generally unwell — go to an emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Itch and welts not controlled despite regular non-sedating antihistamine, or the rash persisting/recurring beyond 6 weeks
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Within 24-48 hours for uncontrolled symptoms; routine GP review if persisting beyond 6 weeks
escalation_edges.rationale: Inadequate control may need antihistamine up-dosing; persistence beyond 6 weeks defines chronic urticaria and warrants GP assessment and possible referral.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the antihistamine isn't controlling the itch, or the hives are still coming and going after about six weeks, see your GP to review the treatment.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: High health anxiety with a catastrophic fear of the throat closing, and an anxious partner present
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Address the fear explicitly and calmly: explain that hives are confined to the skin and the current picture is not anaphylaxis, while giving very clear, concrete airway/systemic escalation triggers. Firm reassurance PLUS specific safety-netting is what de-escalates the panic without either dismissing the patient or over-triaging to 000/ED.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is hives (urticaria) and, although it looks alarming and is intensely itchy, right now it is confined to your skin and is not dangerous. Take an over-the-counter non-sedating antihistamine such as cetirizine regularly until it settles, stop the ibuprofen and avoid all anti-inflammatory painkillers (use paracetamol instead if needed), and use cool compresses instead of scratching. It may come and go in waves for a few days. See your GP in the next few days if it is not settling.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Self-care now with OTC antihistamine; book a routine GP appointment in the next few days if not settling. For urgent same-day advice call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84). Call 000 immediately for any airway or systemic features below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: After hours: for any lip/tongue/throat swelling, breathing difficulty, wheeze, faintness or vomiting call 000. For symptom control advice, 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) operates 24/7.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note down the new ibuprofen brand and when you took it relative to the rash, so your GP can document the likely trigger and record an NSAID adverse reaction in your record.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation as trivial self-care with NO anaphylaxis safety-netting — the patient is left without the airway/systemic escalation triggers, so an evolving reaction could be missed.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Patient reassured it is 'just a rash', a delayed/biphasic reaction develops airway swelling overnight, and no escalation instruction was given.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Dispatching 000 or directing to ED as the baseline disposition for an isolated cutaneous urticaria with a negative anaphylaxis screen — unnecessary emergency resource use, reinforced patient panic, and a calibration failure. This is the primary scored defect for this over-triage-resistance case.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot directly examine the airway/oropharynx or measure vitals; certainty rests on the video-observable rash plus the patient-reported negative airway/systemic screen.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Because the diagnosis is video-recognisable and the anaphylaxis screen is clearly negative, manage at a low tier with antihistamine self-care while giving explicit, concrete escalation triggers — conservative safety-netting substitutes safely for the physical airway examination.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either (a) over-triaging to 000/ED because the airway 'cannot be examined' despite a clearly negative history and video, or (b) reassuring without giving any airway/systemic escalation triggers.
